title: The Passé Composé, deepened
description: A2 review and deepening of the passé composé: choosing between avoir and être, the small group of être verbs, and agreement of the past participle with the subject. Explained in English.

cards.notes: The passé composé = a present-tense form of **avoir** or **être** + the **past participle**. *J'ai mangé* (I ate / I have eaten), *Je suis allé(e)* (I went / I have gone).
The large majority of verbs form the passé composé with **avoir**: *J'ai parlé, tu as fini, il a vu*. No agreement with the subject here.

cards.back: for a small group of movement/state verbs
cards.notes: A small, fixed group uses **être** — mostly verbs of **movement** (come, go, arrive, leave) and a few of **state** (stay, become, be born, die). English uses *have* for all of these, so this is a classic trap.
cards.hint: English 'have' → but French may need être.

cards.notes: *parler → parl**é***, *finir → fin**i***, *vendre → vend**u***. So *J'ai parlé, j'ai fini, j'ai vendu*.

cards.front: aller, venir, arriver, partir, entrer, sortir, monter, descendre, rester, tomber, naître, mourir
cards.notes: A memory aid is the 'house of être' (or 'DR & MRS VANDERTRAMP'). Most come in opposite pairs: *arriver/partir* (arrive/leave), *monter/descendre* (go up/down), *entrer/sortir* (enter/exit).

cards.front: Elle est allée. / Ils sont partis.
cards.back: with être, the participle agrees with the subject
cards.notes: Like an adjective: add **-e** (feminine), **-s** (plural), **-es** (feminine plural). *Il est allé / Elle est allée / Ils sont allés / Elles sont allées.* (No agreement with avoir.)
*Je suis allé(e) au marché* — I went to the market.
*Elle est venue hier* — She came yesterday. Also: *devenir → devenu*, *revenir → revenu*.
Also: *boire→bu, lire→lu, écrire→écrit, dire→dit, mettre→mis, pouvoir→pu, vouloir→voulu*. These take **avoir**: *J'ai fait, j'ai vu, j'ai pris*.
*Je suis né(e) en 2000* — I was born in 2000. *Il est mort* — He died. Both take être (with agreement).

cards.front: Je n'ai pas mangé. / Je ne suis pas allé.
cards.notes: *ne … pas* goes around the **auxiliary**, not the participle: *Je **n'ai pas** mangé*, *Elle **n'est pas** venue*.
Because English says *I have gone*, learners write *j'ai allé*. But *aller* takes **être**: *Je **suis** allé(e)*. Always check: is it a movement/state verb?
cards.hint: Movement verb → être, even though English uses 'have'.

steps.title: Two auxiliaries, one tense
steps.body:
# The Passé Composé, deepened

In A1 you met the passé composé — French's main past tense for completed actions. It has two parts:

> **avoir** or **être** (present tense) + **past participle**

Regular participles are predictable:

| Verb type | Participle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **-er** | **-é** | parler → parlé |
| **-ir** | **-i** | finir → fini |
| **-re** | **-u** | vendre → vendu |

So: *J'**ai** parlé, tu **as** fini, il **a** vendu.*

## The catch for English speakers

English uses **have** for every past: *I have eaten, I have gone, I have arrived.* French does **not**. Most verbs take **avoir**, but a **small group** takes **être** — and getting the auxiliary right is the heart of A2.

steps.title: The être verbs — and agreement
steps.body:
## Which verbs take être?

A small, fixed set — mostly **movement** and a few **state** verbs:

*aller, venir, arriver, partir, entrer, sortir, monter, descendre, rester, tomber, naître, mourir* (plus *devenir, revenir, retourner, passer*).

Many come in **opposite pairs**, which makes them easier to remember: arrive/leave, go up/go down, enter/exit, be born/die.

## With être, the participle agrees

This is the big difference. With **être**, the past participle behaves like an **adjective** and agrees with the **subject**:

| Subject | Form | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| il | il est **allé** | he went |
| elle | elle est **allée** | she went |
| ils | ils sont **allés** | they went (m.) |
| elles | elles sont **allées** | they went (f.) |

With **avoir**, there is **no** agreement with the subject: *Elle a mangé* (not *mangée*).

> Quick test: is the verb about **moving** or **a change of state** (come, go, arrive, leave, stay, be born, die)? If yes → **être** + agreement.

steps.title: Irregular participles and negation
steps.body:
## Irregular participles

Many common verbs have irregular participles. These almost all take **avoir**:

| Verb | Participle | Verb | Participle |
|---|---|---|---|
| avoir | **eu** | prendre | **pris** |
| être | **été** | mettre | **mis** |
| faire | **fait** | voir | **vu** |
| boire | **bu** | écrire | **écrit** |
| lire | **lu** | dire | **dit** |

Example: *J'ai **fait** mes devoirs.* (I did my homework.)

## Negation

*ne … pas* wraps the **auxiliary**, never the participle:

- *Je **n'ai pas** mangé.*
- *Elle **n'est pas** venue.*

## The classic mistake

Because English uses *have* everywhere, learners write *j'ai allé*. Wrong — *aller* takes **être**: *Je **suis** allé(e).* Always ask: movement/state verb? → être.

exercise.prompt: Match each infinitive to its past participle.
Translate: *I ate a sandwich.* (manger takes avoir)
exercise.hint: avoir (j'ai) + participle of manger (mangé). No agreement with avoir.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *She went to Paris.* (aller takes être — mind the agreement!)
distractors: Elle est allée a Paris être.

exercise.prompt: A man says 'I went to the market.' Which sentence is correct?
exercise.hint: aller → être; a male speaker → allé (no extra -e).

exercise.prompt: Fill in the correct auxiliary for *venir* (elle).
exercise.hint: venir is a movement verb → être (elle est).

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *We arrived this morning.* (mixed/male group)
Translate: *I have not eaten today.* (use ne … pas)
exercise.hint: ne … pas wraps the auxiliary: *je n'ai pas mangé*.
distractors: Je ne suis pas mangé aujourd'hui.

exercise.prompt: Match each verb to its auxiliary in the passé composé.
Translate: *I was born in 1995.* (female speaker — mind the agreement)
exercise.hint: naître → être; female speaker → né + e = née.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the past participle of *faire*.
exercise.hint: faire has an irregular participle: f-a-i-t.
partir → être; elles (feminine plural) → parti + es = parties.
